metadata.name: American History (1800s)
metadata.description: The century that defined the United States — westward expansion, the Civil War, Reconstruction, industrialization, and the era of robber barons and reform.
metadata.author: Trivial World Team

questions.questionText: President Jefferson purchased a vast territory from France in 1803 that roughly doubled the size of the United States. What was this land deal called?
questions.tidbits: The Louisiana Purchase cost approximately $15 million — about 3 cents per acre — for roughly 830,000 square miles.

questions.questionText: What overland route, stretching about 2,000 miles from Missouri to Oregon, carried hundreds of thousands of settlers westward from the 1840s onward?
questions.tidbits: The Oregon Trail was so heavily traveled that wagon wheel ruts are still visible in the landscape of Wyoming and Nebraska today.

questions.questionText: Which US state was the site of the 1848 gold discovery that triggered the California Gold Rush?
questions.tidbits: Gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill in Coloma, California on January 24, 1848.

questions.questionText: The 1848 Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, ending the Mexican-American War, transferred which large region to the United States?
questions.answerText: The Mexican Cession (present-day California, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, Colorado, Wyoming)
questions.tidbits: The Mexican Cession added over 525,000 square miles and accelerated the debate over whether new states would allow slavery.

questions.questionText: The transcontinental railroad, completed in 1869, linked the east to the west. Where did the two rail lines meet to drive in the golden spike?
questions.tidbits: The ceremony on May 10, 1869 was the first national event transmitted coast-to-coast by telegraph.

questions.questionText: The forced removal of the Cherokee Nation from their southeastern homelands to present-day Oklahoma in 1838–1839 became known by what name?
questions.tidbits: Approximately 4,000 of the 15,000 Cherokee who were forced to march died from hunger, disease, and exposure.

questions.questionText: Which 1853 land purchase from Mexico added the southernmost strip of present-day Arizona and New Mexico, completing the continental US boundary?
questions.tidbits: The Gadsden Purchase was negotiated to secure a southern route for a transcontinental railroad.

questions.questionText: The Missouri Compromise of 1820 admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, and drew a slavery dividing line at what latitude?
questions.tidbits: The Missouri Compromise held for 34 years until the Kansas-Nebraska Act of 1854 effectively repealed it.

questions.questionText: Which Union general accepted Robert E. Lee's surrender at Appomattox Court House on April 9, 1865?
questions.tidbits: Grant allowed Confederate soldiers to keep their horses and sidearms, a generous gesture that helped ease the transition to peace.

questions.questionText: The Homestead Act of 1862 offered settlers how many acres of public land in exchange for improving it over five years?
questions.tidbits: The Homestead Act eventually transferred approximately 270 million acres — 10% of the United States — to private individuals.

questions.questionText: Which president issued the Emancipation Proclamation on January 1, 1863, declaring enslaved people in Confederate states to be free?
questions.tidbits: The Emancipation Proclamation changed the character of the war, making the abolition of slavery an explicit Union goal.

questions.questionText: Which formerly enslaved woman became the most famous conductor of the Underground Railroad, leading dozens of people to freedom?
questions.tidbits: Harriet Tubman made approximately 13 missions to rescue around 70 enslaved people, and said she never lost a passenger.

questions.questionText: Which abolitionist and orator, born into slavery, became one of the most influential voices for freedom and equality in the 1800s?
questions.tidbits: Frederick Douglass's autobiography, 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass,' became a seminal anti-slavery text.

questions.questionText: Which suffragist, along with Elizabeth Cady Stanton, organized the first women's rights convention in Seneca Falls, New York, in 1848?
questions.answerText: Susan B. Anthony (co-organizer; the convention was organized by Stanton, Lucretia Mott, and others — Anthony joined the movement shortly after)
questions.tidbits: The Seneca Falls Convention produced the Declaration of Sentiments, modeled on the Declaration of Independence, demanding equal rights for women.

questions.questionText: Which Confederate general surrendered at Appomattox and became a symbol of the 'Lost Cause' mythology after the Civil War?
questions.tidbits: After the war, Lee became president of Washington College (now Washington and Lee University) in Lexington, Virginia.

questions.questionText: Which industrialist, known as the 'Steel King,' dominated the American steel industry and later gave away most of his fortune to build libraries and fund education?
questions.tidbits: Carnegie gave away over $350 million during his lifetime — equivalent to roughly $6 billion today — funding over 2,500 libraries worldwide.

questions.questionText: Which Lakota Sioux chief was a key leader at the Battle of Little Bighorn in 1876, where Custer's 7th Cavalry was defeated?
questions.tidbits: Sitting Bull had a vision of soldiers falling from the sky before the battle, which he interpreted as a sign of victory.

questions.questionText: Which Missouri-born author wrote 'Adventures of Huckleberry Finn' in 1884, a novel that explored race and freedom along the Mississippi River?
questions.tidbits: Ernest Hemingway said all modern American literature comes from 'Adventures of Huckleberry Finn.'

questions.questionText: Which oil tycoon founded Standard Oil in 1870 and used horizontal integration to control approximately 90% of US oil refining by the 1880s?
questions.tidbits: Standard Oil was broken up by the Supreme Court in 1911 under the Sherman Antitrust Act into 34 separate companies.

questions.questionText: Which Nez Perce chief led his people on a 1,170-mile retreat across the Rocky Mountains in 1877 before surrendering with the famous words 'I will fight no more forever'?
questions.tidbits: Chief Joseph's retreat is studied as a military masterpiece — his outnumbered band evaded US Army forces for over three months.

questions.questionText: What war, fought between 1861 and 1865, pitted the Union against the Confederacy over slavery and states' rights?
questions.tidbits: The Civil War resulted in approximately 620,000 to 750,000 soldier deaths — more than any other war in American history.

questions.questionText: Which 1865 constitutional amendment abolished slavery throughout the United States?
questions.tidbits: The 13th Amendment was ratified on December 6, 1865 — eight months after the Civil War ended.

questions.questionText: The Civil War began when Confederate forces fired on which Union fort in South Carolina in April 1861?
questions.tidbits: Remarkably, no soldiers died in the bombardment of Fort Sumter itself — the first Union casualties came from an accidental explosion during the surrender ceremony.

questions.questionText: Which 1857 Supreme Court ruling declared that enslaved people were not citizens and that Congress had no power to ban slavery in territories?
questions.tidbits: The Dred Scott decision is widely considered one of the worst in Supreme Court history and helped push the nation toward civil war.

questions.questionText: Which three-day battle in Pennsylvania in July 1863 is considered the turning point of the Civil War, resulting in a Union victory?
questions.tidbits: The Battle of Gettysburg resulted in approximately 50,000 casualties in three days — the bloodiest battle of the Civil War.

questions.questionText: President Monroe's 1823 declaration warning European powers not to interfere in the affairs of the Western Hemisphere became known as what doctrine?
questions.tidbits: The Monroe Doctrine was largely unenforceable when proclaimed but became a cornerstone of American foreign policy throughout the century.

questions.questionText: What was the bloodiest single day in American military history, occurring during the Battle of Antietam in September 1862?
questions.tidbits: Lincoln used the Union's 'victory' at Antietam as the opportunity to issue the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation.

questions.questionText: The Kansas-Nebraska Act of 1854 introduced what concept that allowed settlers in new territories to vote on whether to allow slavery?
questions.tidbits: The resulting conflict in Kansas between pro-slavery and anti-slavery settlers became known as 'Bleeding Kansas.'

questions.questionText: Which constitutional amendments, ratified during Reconstruction, granted citizenship to formerly enslaved people and voting rights to Black men?
questions.answerText: 14th Amendment (1868, citizenship) and 15th Amendment (1870, voting rights)
questions.tidbits: Despite the 15th Amendment, Southern states used poll taxes, literacy tests, and violence to disenfranchise Black voters for nearly a century.

questions.questionText: The Spanish-American War of 1898 was partly triggered by the mysterious explosion of what US Navy ship in Havana Harbor?
questions.tidbits: 'Remember the Maine, to hell with Spain!' became the rallying cry for American intervention, though the explosion's cause was never definitively proven.

questions.questionText: Which 1852 anti-slavery novel by Harriet Beecher Stowe depicted the brutal realities of slavery and became a massive bestseller?
questions.tidbits: When Lincoln met Stowe in 1862, he reportedly said, 'So you are the little woman who wrote the book that started this great war.'

questions.questionText: What term, coined by Mark Twain and Charles Dudley Warner for their 1873 novel, describes the era of rapid economic growth and widespread corruption after the Civil War?
questions.tidbits: 'Gilded' means covered in a thin layer of gold — a surface shine concealing corruption and inequality underneath.

questions.questionText: Walt Whitman's 1855 poetry collection, which celebrated American democracy and the individual, is considered a cornerstone of American literature. What is it called?
questions.tidbits: Whitman self-published the first edition of 'Leaves of Grass' and continued revising it throughout his life, producing nine editions total.

questions.questionText: Which Massachusetts-born author and philosopher, along with Henry David Thoreau, led the Transcendentalist movement in the mid-1800s?
questions.tidbits: Emerson's essay 'Self-Reliance' called on individuals to trust their own instincts and reject conformity — ideas that shaped American individualism.

questions.questionText: Which Black abolitionist newspaper, published by Frederick Douglass starting in 1847, became one of the most influential anti-slavery publications in America?
questions.answerText: The North Star (later Frederick Douglass' Paper)
questions.tidbits: The North Star's motto was 'Right is of no Sex — Truth is of no Color — God is the Father of us all, and we are all Brethren.'

questions.questionText: Which Massachusetts author wrote 'Walden,' an account of his two-year experiment in simple living in the woods near Concord?
questions.tidbits: Thoreau's essay 'Civil Disobedience' influenced later activists including Mahatma Gandhi and Martin Luther King Jr.

questions.questionText: Lincoln's brief but powerful speech dedicating the military cemetery at Gettysburg is known as what?
questions.tidbits: The Gettysburg Address is just 272 words long, yet is considered one of the greatest speeches in American history.

questions.questionText: Which New England poet, known for her reclusive life and unconventional dashes and slant rhyme, wrote nearly 1,800 poems that were mostly published after her death in 1886?
questions.tidbits: Only about a dozen of Dickinson's poems were published during her lifetime; her sister discovered the full collection after her death.

questions.questionText: What is the term for the political philosophy that the United States was divinely destined to expand across the North American continent to the Pacific Ocean?
questions.tidbits: The phrase 'Manifest Destiny' was coined by journalist John O'Sullivan in 1845 to advocate for the annexation of Texas.

questions.questionText: Which abolitionist newspaper was founded by William Lloyd Garrison in 1831 in Boston and became one of the most influential anti-slavery publications for 35 years?
questions.tidbits: Garrison's first editorial declared he would be 'as harsh as truth, and as uncompromising as justice' in demanding immediate emancipation.

questions.questionText: Who invented the telegraph and the Morse code system that made long-distance communication possible in the 1840s?
questions.tidbits: The first long-distance telegraph message, sent from Washington D.C. to Baltimore in 1844, was 'What hath God wrought?'

questions.questionText: Who invented the practical incandescent light bulb in 1879, ushering in the age of electric lighting?
questions.tidbits: Edison also developed a complete electrical distribution system, opening the first commercial power station on Pearl Street in New York City in 1882.

questions.questionText: Who is credited with inventing the telephone in 1876?
questions.tidbits: Bell's first telephone call was to his assistant Thomas Watson: 'Mr. Watson — come here — I want to see you.'

questions.questionText: What ironmaking process, developed in the 1850s by Henry Bessemer, dramatically reduced the cost of producing steel and enabled the construction of skyscrapers and railroads?
questions.tidbits: The Bessemer Process reduced the time to make steel from days to under 20 minutes, dropping the price of steel by 80%.

questions.questionText: What two railroad companies built the transcontinental railroad from opposite ends, meeting at Promontory Summit in 1869?
questions.answerText: Union Pacific (building westward) and Central Pacific (building eastward)
questions.tidbits: The Central Pacific relied heavily on Chinese immigrant labor — approximately 10,000 Chinese workers who often faced dangerous conditions.

questions.questionText: John D. Rockefeller used what business structure — controlling all parts of an industry from raw material to retail — to dominate the oil industry?
questions.answerText: Vertical integration (plus horizontal integration to eliminate competitors)
questions.tidbits: Standard Oil at its peak controlled 90% of US oil refining and set the template for modern corporate monopoly.

questions.questionText: What 1862 law granted free land to settlers who agreed to farm it for five years, opening vast territories to westward expansion?
questions.tidbits: Between 1862 and 1934, about 1.6 million people filed Homestead Act claims, and roughly 783,000 succeeded in gaining title.

questions.questionText: What invention by Cyrus McCormick in 1831 mechanized grain harvesting and transformed American agriculture, helping feed rapidly growing cities?
questions.tidbits: McCormick's reaper did the work of five men, enabling single farmers to harvest much larger fields and feeding the Union Army during the Civil War.

questions.questionText: What federal law, passed in 1890, gave the government the power to break up monopolies that restrained trade — and was used to dismantle Standard Oil in 1911?
questions.tidbits: The Sherman Antitrust Act was the first federal law to limit cartels and monopolies, but was weakly enforced until Theodore Roosevelt's presidency.

questions.questionText: Lewis and Clark's Corps of Discovery expedition explored the Louisiana Purchase territory from 1804 to 1806, guided in part by which Native American woman?
questions.tidbits: Sacagawea, a Shoshone woman, was invaluable as an interpreter and her presence helped signal to Native groups that the expedition was peaceful.

questions.questionText: What famous battle at a Texas mission in 1836 ended with the deaths of all its defenders, including Davy Crockett and Jim Bowie, during the Texas Revolution?
questions.tidbits: 'Remember the Alamo!' became the rallying cry that helped Sam Houston defeat Santa Anna at the Battle of San Jacinto weeks later.

questions.questionText: Which US Army officer was killed along with all 210 men under his immediate command at the Battle of Little Bighorn in 1876?
questions.tidbits: The Battle of Little Bighorn is also called Custer's Last Stand — the combined Sioux and Cheyenne forces numbered around 1,500–2,000 warriors.

questions.questionText: What was the name of Theodore Roosevelt's volunteer cavalry unit that fought in Cuba during the Spanish-American War of 1898?
questions.answerText: The Rough Riders (1st United States Volunteer Cavalry)
questions.tidbits: The Rough Riders' charge up Kettle Hill (often called San Juan Hill) made Roosevelt a national war hero and launched his political career.

questions.questionText: Which war, fought between 1846 and 1848, resulted in the United States acquiring California, Nevada, Utah, and parts of several other states from Mexico?
questions.tidbits: The war was opposed by many Americans including Abraham Lincoln and Henry David Thoreau, who wrote 'Civil Disobedience' partly in protest.

questions.questionText: At which Virginia courthouse did Robert E. Lee formally surrender to Ulysses S. Grant on April 9, 1865, effectively ending the Civil War?
questions.tidbits: The surrender took place in the parlor of Wilmer McLean's house — the same man who had fled his first home near Bull Run to escape the war.

questions.questionText: Which December 1890 massacre marked the end of armed Native American resistance on the Great Plains, killing approximately 250 to 300 Lakota Sioux?
questions.tidbits: Wounded Knee was the last major armed conflict between the US Army and Native Americans, occurring just weeks after Sitting Bull was killed.

questions.questionText: What war between the US and Britain, triggered partly by British impressment of American sailors, was fought from 1812 to 1815?
questions.tidbits: During the War of 1812, British forces burned the White House and Capitol Building in Washington D.C. in 1814.

questions.questionText: Which Civil War battle in September 1862, fought along Antietam Creek in Maryland, was the single bloodiest day in American military history with approximately 22,700 casualties?
questions.tidbits: The tactical draw at Antietam was enough for Lincoln to claim a Union victory and issue the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation five days later.

questions.questionText: Which 1830 federal law, signed by President Andrew Jackson, authorized the forced relocation of Native American tribes from the southeastern US to west of the Mississippi River?
questions.tidbits: The Indian Removal Act displaced over 100,000 Native Americans, leading to the Trail of Tears and other forced marches with devastating mortality.

questions.questionText: Confederate General Thomas Jackson earned what famous nickname after holding his position firmly at the First Battle of Bull Run in 1861?
questions.tidbits: Jackson was accidentally shot by his own troops at Chancellorsville in 1863 and died of pneumonia eight days later — a devastating loss for the Confederacy.
